us-politics.headline: US Warplanes Shot Down Over Iran, Crew Missing
us-politics.body:
Two US fighter jets were shot down during operations in Iran, with at least one crew member still missing as recovery efforts continue. Two helicopters were also hit during the search, raising the human and military cost of the conflict. The incidents mark a serious escalation.

On Capitol Hill, Republican Senator John Curtis called for ending offensive operations without a formal declaration of war, breaking with the administration's posture. The episode is sharpening a long-running constitutional debate over presidential war powers. No Democrat or Republican has yet moved formal legislation to halt the campaign.

sources.title: What to know about US fighter jets hit in Iran war
2 US helicopters hit during recovery efforts in Iran
GOP senator calls to end Iran operations without formal declaration of war
Two U.S. warplanes shot down; search ongoing in Iran for 1 missing crew member

us-politics.headline: Trump Reshapes DOJ, Budget, and Cabinet Amid Turbulence
us-politics.body:
Todd Blanche, Trump's former personal attorney, has taken over as the Justice Department's top official, stepping into a role carrying heavy political expectations. Blanche told reporters there is no evidence in the Epstein files that Epstein worked as a spy. Epstein victims separately demanded a meeting with King Charles during his Washington visit.

Trump's budget proposal drew bipartisan friction. Republican Senator Susan Collins publicly criticized funding cuts, while Senate Democrats pressed Treasury over plans to put Trump's name on US currency. Trump also proposed a $10 billion fund for Washington DC construction projects. Reports indicate he is weighing further Cabinet changes but wants to avoid a broad shake-up.

sources.title: Epstein victims demand meeting with King Charles in Washington
Trump weighs more Cabinet changes, but wants to avoid ‘massive shake-up’
Todd Blanche takes on DOJ’s top job and the burden of Trump’s expectations
Susan Collins knocks funding cuts in White House budget proposal
What to know about Trump’s defense budget proposal
Blanche: No evidence in Epstein files he was a spy
Senate Democrats press Treasury Department on decision to add Trump name to currency
Trump proposes $10B fund for D.C. construction, beautification projects

us-politics.headline: Trump Signs College Sports Order, Issues Good Friday Message
us-politics.body:
Trump signed an executive order targeting college sports reform, his second such directive aimed at reshaping NCAA governance. The order focuses on athlete compensation and competitive fairness, though critics note previous orders in this area produced limited results. Supporters say federal pressure is the only lever strong enough to force change.

On Good Friday, Trump released a message declaring "America needs God" and pointing to what he called a resurgence of religious faith in the country. The statement drew praise from conservative commentators and skepticism from those who see it as political signaling ahead of the Easter weekend.

sources.title: Trump signs order aimed at reforming college sports
Trump says ‘America needs God’ in Good Friday message touting ‘resurgence of religion'
Trump again orders changes to ‘save’ college sports

us-politics.headline: Oregon Sues Over Mail Voting; Colorado Lawyers Flag ICE Pledge
us-politics.body:
Oregon's attorney general sued the Trump administration over an executive order restricting vote-by-mail, calling it a source of "confusion, chaos and distrust." Oregon conducts all elections by mail and argues the order is an unconstitutional federal intrusion into state election administration. The case is likely to move quickly through the courts.

In Colorado, lawyers using the state court's electronic filing system reported being required to certify they will not assist ICE. The requirement sparked immediate backlash from attorneys who argue it conflicts with federal law and professional obligations. Both disputes reflect widening state-federal friction over immigration and election policy.

sources.title: ‘Confusion, chaos and distrust’: Oregon challenges Trump’s order restricting vote-by-mail
Colorado lawyers say court e-file system now makes them certify they won’t assist ICE

au-politics.headline: Pezzullo Report: Former Home Affairs Boss Labelled 'Reckless'
au-politics.body:
A confidential report has found former Home Affairs secretary Michael Pezzullo acted recklessly in his dealings with a Liberal powerbroker, adding fresh scrutiny to one of Australia's most senior public servants. Pezzullo was sacked in 2023 after an investigation into text messages exchanged with Liberal operative Scott Briggs.

The findings raise ongoing questions about the boundaries between the public service and political networks. Critics argue the case exposes systemic vulnerabilities in how senior bureaucrats engage with party figures. Supporters of Pezzullo have previously argued his record of service deserves fuller consideration. The report remains confidential, limiting independent scrutiny of its conclusions.

sources.title: Confidential report found former home affairs boss Michael Pezzullo was ‘reckless’ in engagement with Liberal powerbroker

au-politics.headline: Major Drug Bust: Nine Charged Over 'Mother Ship' Smuggling Plot
au-politics.body:
Nine people face charges over an alleged conspiracy to import tonnes of cocaine and methamphetamine into Australia using a vessel operating as a so-called mother ship in Australian waters. The operation unravelled after a trawler sank, alerting police to the alleged syndicate.

Victoria Police coordinated the investigation, which points to a sophisticated transnational smuggling network. The scale of the alleged importation - measured in tonnes - places it among the more significant drug interceptions in recent years. Authorities have not yet detailed the full chain of alleged participants or the drugs' intended distribution network.

sources.title: Nine charged over alleged conspiracy to import tonnes of cocaine and meth via ‘mother ship’ in Australian waters
Sunken trawler alerts police to alleged drug-smuggling plan

au-politics.headline: Fuel Supply Fears Squeeze Farmers as Supermarkets Face Pressure
au-politics.body:
Australia's energy minister says fuel supplies remain strong and fewer service stations are running dry, but the reassurance has not quieted concern across the agricultural sector. Farmers are calling on supermarkets to pay more for produce to help absorb rising fuel costs hitting transport and farm operations.

The tension sits inside a broader debate about supermarket pricing power and the squeeze on primary producers. Farmers argue thin margins leave them exposed when input costs spike. Supermarkets have not publicly committed to price adjustments. The government's position - that supply is stable - contrasts with the on-the-ground anxiety driving panic buying at some outlets.

sources.title: Fewer service stations running out of petrol and diesel as Australia’s fuel supplies remain strong, energy minister says
Supermarkets asked to pay more for produce to absorb fuel costs for farmers

au-politics.headline: Victorian Election Takes Shape as Challenger Targets Premier Allan
au-politics.body:
Andrew Lethlean has confirmed he will contest the seat of Bendigo East against Victorian Premier Jacinta Allan at the next state election, setting up a direct challenge to the state's most senior Labor figure outside of cabinet. Lethlean's candidacy signals the opposition is targeting Allan's seat as a symbolic prize.

Allan has held Bendigo East since 2002 and leads a government navigating cost-of-living pressure and infrastructure cost blowouts. A successful challenge would be a significant upset. The move reflects broader opposition strategy to stretch Labor's resources and put the premier on the defensive in her own electorate ahead of a campaign that is still taking shape.

sources.title: Lethlean to challenge Victorian premier in Bendigo East

world.headline: US-Israel War on Iran Enters Day 36 With Escalating Strikes
world.body:
American and Israeli forces continued strikes on Iran for a 36th consecutive day, while Tehran downed two US warplanes and launched missile attacks on residential areas in central Israel. Iran also executed two members of a banned opposition group amid the ongoing conflict, a move critics say is designed to suppress internal dissent under cover of war.

Israel simultaneously bombed bridges in Lebanon, widening the regional footprint of the conflict. A rescue team in Iran is conducting what officials describe as a harrowing search for a missing US crew member, adding a new dimension to an already volatile situation. The war is reshaping alliances and drawing in neighbouring states at speed.

sources.title: Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member
Iran executes two convicted members of banned opposition group
Iran war: What is happening on day 36 of US-Israeli attacks?
Iranian missile attack hits residential areas in central Israel
Iran war live: Tehran downs 2 US warplanes; Israel bombs Lebanon bridges

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Reaches Moon's Halfway Point, Shares Earth Images
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew passed the halfway mark to the Moon, capturing what mission controllers called spectacular images of Earth from deep space. Both NASA and Al Jazeera published the photographs, which show the planet in full against the black of space - the first such images from a crewed lunar mission in decades.

The mission marks a significant return to human deep-space travel. The crew remains on track for a lunar flyby before returning to Earth.

sources.title: Artemis II crew now halfway to Moon as they take 'spectacular' image of Earth
NASA unveils first images of Earth from Artemis II

world.headline: Trump Opens US Wilderness to Drilling as Europe Eyes Nuclear
world.body:
The Trump administration moved to open protected wilderness areas to oil and gas extraction, advancing its drill baby drill energy agenda through executive action. Environmental groups condemned the decision, warning of irreversible damage to federal lands.

Across the Atlantic, Europe is asking a different question - whether restarting or expanding nuclear power is the answer to a fresh energy shock driven by volatile gas prices and supply insecurity. The two approaches reflect a widening transatlantic split on how to secure energy in an unstable world.

sources.title: 'Drill baby drill': Trump opens wilderness to big energy
Faced with new energy shock, Europe asks if reviving nuclear is the answer

world.headline: Hungary's Orban Faces Strongest Political Challenge in 16 Years
world.body:
Viktor Orban, Hungary's dominant leader since 2010, is facing the most serious electoral threat of his tenure. A unified opposition and a charismatic challenger have shifted polling in ways that would have seemed implausible just a year ago, raising the prospect of a genuine contest for power in Budapest.

Orban has survived repeated predictions of his downfall by consolidating media, courts, and electoral rules in his favour. Whether those structural advantages hold against a newly energised opposition remains the central question heading into the campaign.

sources.title: After 16 years in power, could Viktor Orban finally be unseated?

ai.headline: Anthropic Dominates Headlines With Research, Acquisitions, and Politics
ai.body:
Anthropic is moving fast on multiple fronts. The company acquired biotech startup Coefficient Bio for $400 million, launched a new political action committee, and published research showing Claude develops functional emotion-like concepts internally. Claude Code also made news after independently uncovering a Linux vulnerability that had gone undetected for 23 years.

On the business side, Anthropic is riding high in private markets - though TechCrunch notes a looming SpaceX IPO could divert investor appetite. The company also drew criticism for effectively blocking OpenClaw, a third-party Claude client, by requiring subscribers to pay extra to use it. Security researchers separately warned OpenClaw users to assume their accounts may be compromised following a separate breach.

sources.title: Emotion concepts and their function in a large language model
Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Claude Code Found a Linux Vulnerability Hidden for 23 Years
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw
OpenClaw gives users yet another reason to be freaked out about security
Anthropic buys biotech startup Coefficient Bio in $400M deal: Reports
Anthropic ramps up its political activities with a new PAC

ai.headline: OpenAI Shuffles Executives as Leadership Instability Continues
ai.body:
OpenAI is reshuffling its executive ranks again. COO Brad Lightcap is moving into a new "special projects" role, while Fidji Simo is taking medical leave shortly after joining. The company's AGI division head is also stepping back. The simultaneous departures raise questions about internal cohesion at a company already navigating a contentious restructuring.

The moves come as OpenAI faces intensifying competition from Anthropic and growing scrutiny over its governance.

sources.title: OpenAI executive shuffle includes new role for COO Brad Lightcap to lead ‘special projects’
OpenAI’s AGI boss is taking a leave of absence
OpenAI’s Fidji Simo Is Taking Medical Leave Amid an Executive Shake-Up

ai.headline: AI's Energy Hunger Collides With Political and Public Resistance
ai.body:
The infrastructure behind AI is generating its own crisis. AI companies are building large natural gas plants to power data centers, drawing environmental concern, while a new survey finds people would sooner accept an Amazon warehouse nearby than a data center. Trump's tariffs are meanwhile undermining his own AI data center push by raising construction costs.

Elon Musk added a separate wrinkle, reportedly demanding that banks working on the SpaceX IPO purchase Grok subscriptions - a move that blurs the line between business leverage and coercion. Research published this week also found that heavy AI use is pushing users toward "cognitive surrender," with people increasingly outsourcing their reasoning to models rather than thinking independently.

sources.title: Elon Musk insists banks working on SpaceX IPO must buy Grok subscriptions
"Cognitive surrender" leads AI users to abandon logical thinking, research finds
Trump ignores biggest reasons his AI data center buildout is failing
AI companies are building huge natural gas plants to power data centers. What could go wrong?
People would rather have an Amazon warehouse in their backyard than a data center

ai.headline: Artemis II Heads to the Moon as NASA Budget Faces Cuts
ai.body:
The Artemis II crew is en route to the Moon and sending back striking images of Earth, with the mission described as running smoothly so far. The timing is awkward for the White House - Trump has simultaneously proposed steep cuts to NASA's budget, threatening the agency's long-term capacity even as its most ambitious crewed mission in decades unfolds.

The contrast is sharp. Astronauts are circling the Moon while the administration moves to defund the program that put them there.

sources.title: Trump proposes steep cut to NASA budget as astronauts head for the Moon
As Artemis II zooms to the Moon, everything seems to be going swimmingly
Artemis II crew take “spectacular” image of Earth

btc.headline: Bitcoin Consolidates as ETF Dominance Over Gold Looms
btc.body:
Bitcoin is grinding sideways, but analysts say the directionless price action may be coiling toward a sharp breakout rather than a breakdown. Tight consolidation after a volatile stretch often precedes outsized moves in either direction.

Meanwhile, the longer-term bull case keeps building. Bloomberg analyst James Seyffart argues Bitcoin ETFs will eventually surpass gold ETFs in size, pointing to Bitcoin's expanding use cases beyond a simple store of value. Gold ETFs took decades to reach their current scale. Bitcoin ETFs are moving faster.

sources.title: Bitcoin’s ‘no direction’ action may lead to heavier breakout: Analyst
Bitcoin ETFs 'will be larger' than gold ETFs: Analyst

btc.headline: Tether Eyes $500B Valuation but May Blink on Timing
btc.body:
Tether is exploring a fundraising round at a $500 billion valuation, but the stablecoin giant may shelve the effort if investor demand falls short of expectations. That figure would make it one of the most valuable private financial companies on earth.

The hesitation signals that even dominant players in crypto are reading the room carefully. Tether generated over $13 billion in profit in 2024, so the raise is about strategic positioning rather than necessity. Timing, not fundamentals, is the question.

sources.title: Tether may delay fundraising if demand falls short at $500B valuation: Report

btc.headline: Quantum Threat to Bitcoin Is Real but Still Distant
btc.body:
A claim that quantum computers could crack Bitcoin in nine minutes has circulated widely, but the reality is more nuanced. Current quantum machines lack the qubit stability and scale needed to threaten Bitcoin's cryptography in any practical sense. The nine-minute figure comes from theoretical modeling, not a working attack.

That said, the research is a legitimate warning shot. Bitcoin's elliptic curve signatures are the most vulnerable layer, and the network will eventually need a migration to quantum-resistant cryptography. The window to act is open, but it will not stay open forever.

sources.title: Here's what 'cracking' bitcoin in 9 minutes by quantum computers actually means

btc.headline: Ethereum Foundation Closes In on 70,000 Staked ETH
btc.body:
The Ethereum Foundation is nearly at its self-imposed target of 70,000 staked ETH, a milestone that marks a shift in how the organization engages with the network it helped build. For years the Foundation held back from staking, wary of signaling undue influence over validator sets.

Reaching this goal matters beyond the numbers. It demonstrates institutional confidence in Ethereum's proof-of-stake infrastructure and adds meaningful weight to network security. It also puts pressure on other large ETH holders to participate rather than sit on idle assets.

sources.title: Ethereum Foundation nearly reaches 70,000 staked ETH goal

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Walnut Tart
food.description: A buttery shortcrust tart filled with slow-caramelised leeks, toasted walnuts, and a white miso custard that balances umami with subtle sweetness.
food.tip: Blind-bake the pastry shell for 12 minutes before adding the filling — miso custard is wetter than it looks and a soggy base will ruin the contrast of textures.

exercise.name: The Deck of Cards Workout
exercise.equipment: A standard deck of playing cards, exercise mat
exercise.description: Assign a bodyweight movement to each suit — push-ups, squats, lunges, and hollow-body holds — then flip cards one at a time and perform the number of reps shown, working through the entire deck without a fixed rest structure so intensity is governed by chance rather than willpower.

learn.description: Stochastic resonance is the counterintuitive phenomenon where adding a precise amount of random noise to a system actually improves its ability to detect weak signals — observed in everything from crayfish mechanoreceptors to human tactile perception and financial models.
learn.why: It quietly dismantles the assumption that noise is always the enemy of clarity, and once you understand it you start spotting its implications in neuroscience, engineering, and decision-making in ways that genuinely shift how you think about uncertainty.
